coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for extensive production of hydrogen: A hybrid density functional study
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Mandal, Bikash
Samal, Prasanjit
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic water splitting has emerged as a promising approach for storing solar energy in the form of chemical energy, which can solve the energy crisis and environmental issues. Herein we have explored the potentiality of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for solar-driven hydrogen evolution. The motivation behind applying quantum dots as a photocatalyst is to utilize a major portion of the solar energy spectrum to enhance hydrogen yield. The alignment of frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) in comparison to the redox potential of water reveals that all the studied quantum dots are eligible for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. The energy gap between FMOs varies with the size of quantum dots from 3.01 to 2.32 eV, showing absorption of light in the visible range. The Gibbs free energy calculation shows that the formation of intermediate species is energetically unfavorable without exposure to light at pH=7, except for hydrogen adsorption. However, the photogenerated electron and hole drive the steps of oxygen and hydrogen evolution by making those a downhill process. The optical absorption spectra show that all the studied quantum dots absorb visible light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Hybrid density functional study
Photocatalytic water splitting
Gibbs free energy change
Optical absorption spectrum
Solar-driven hydrogen evolution

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: B.M. is grateful to the Department of Atomic Energy, Government of India for the financial support. We strongly acknowledge the Kalinga Cluster of NISER Bhubaneswar for providing enormous computational support.

B.M. is grateful to the Department of Atomic Energy, Government of India for the financial support. We strongly acknowledge the Kalinga Cluster of NISER Bhubaneswar for providing enormous computational support. The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
The optimized structure of nitrogen-doped (a) quasi-trigonal [NGQD(28)] and (b) hexagonal [NGQD(36)] graphene quantum dots. S
1
and S
2
designate two different sites for OER. H, C, and N are demonstrated by the white, gray, and blue atoms, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The projected density of states (calculated at the HSE06 level) of the smallest studied nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot. The red (magenta) and blue (dotted green) lines show the contribution of
p
z
(s,
p
x
,
p
y
) orbital of carbon and nitrogen, respectively. The Fermi energy is set to zero. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The alignment of frontier molecular orbitals compared to the redox potential of water at p
H=7, which is represented by dotted lines. FMO energies are computed with HSE06 functional. The numbers within parentheses specify the number of carbon atoms in quantum dots.

Schematic diagram of (a) oxidation (at S
1
site) and (b) reduction of water, which lead to overall water splitting. For the sake of simplicity, only a portion of NGQD(28) is shown in the figure, which contains all the active sites in the quantum dot.

The free energy change for the formation of intermediates involved in (a) OER (at S
1
site) and (b) HER on the surface of NGQD(28). The reaction energy at DFT level is computed using HSE06 functional.

The Gibbs free energy change for the formation of intermediates involved in OER at the S
2
site of NGQD(28). The reaction energy based on DFT is calculated applying HSE06 functional.
The optical absorption spectra (calculated applying HSE06 functional) of all the studied nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for extensive production of hydrogen: A hybrid density functional study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Sciences, National Institute of Science Education and Research, Bhubaneswar, Khurda, Jatni, Odisha 752050, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Sciences, National Institute of Science Education and Research, Bhubaneswar

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physical Sciences, NISER Bhubaneswar, Khurda, Jatni, Odisha 752050, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic water splitting has emerged as a promising approach for storing solar energy in the form of chemical energy, which can solve the energy crisis and environmental issues. Herein we have explored the potentiality of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for solar-driven hydrogen evolution. The motivation behind applying quantum dots as a photocatalyst is to utilize a major portion of the solar energy spectrum to enhance hydrogen yield. The alignment of frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) in comparison to the redox potential of water reveals that all the studied quantum dots are eligible for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. The energy gap between FMOs varies with the size of quantum dots from 3.01 to 2.32 eV, showing absorption of light in the visible range. The Gibbs free energy calculation shows that the formation of intermediate species is energetically unfavorable without exposure to light at pH=7, except for hydrogen adsorption. However, the photogenerated electron and hole drive the steps of oxygen and hydrogen evolution by making those a downhill process. The optical absorption spectra show that all the studied quantum dots absorb visible light.

ce:section.ce:para:
The increasing demand for fossil fuels is a great concern for human civilization. On the other hand, the burning of fossil fuels produces harmful gases like CO
2
, NO
2
, SO
2
, etc., which are responsible for the greenhouse effect and subsequent climate change [1]. So, scientists are searching for alternate and renewable sources of energy. Although a great deal of energy is supplied through photovoltaic devices, but still need to fulfill the requirement of energy in human civilization [2]. One possible strategy is the conversion and storage of solar energy in the form of chemical energy through solar fuel production [3,4]. Solar energy can be utilized to produce solar fuel like hydrogen, which can be used as per necessity in fuel cell technology to get electrical energy with an environment-friendly by-product, water. So, this can be an efficient technique for the production of clean, environment-friendly, carbon-free fuel for meeting the demand for energy in human civilization.

The splitting of water has emerged as an effective way for the sustainable production of hydrogen [5]. However, solar energy is not sufficient to perform this transformation. Photocatalysts have attracted severe attention for solar-driven water splitting [5–17]. Inspired by the novel experiment of Fujishima and Honda on photoelectrochemical water splitting [18], a lot of photocatalysts were developed for solar energy conversion, such as TiO
2
, C
3
N
4
, CdS, etc [6,7]. Two-dimensional carbonaceous materials, such as graphene oxide, carbon nitride, carbon quantum dot, etc., have emerged as potential photocatalysts for water splitting due to their unique properties like excellent carrier mobility, high surface area, tunable energy levels, etc [19–25]. The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate and quantum efficiency are reported for various material systems, such as oxides, sulfides, nitrides, phosphides, etc [6,7]. The 1T form of MoSe
2
(MoS
2
) shows excellent hydrogen evolution activity with the rate of around 62 (26) mmol h−1 g−1 [26,27]. Xu et al. [28] explore visible-light-driven water splitting activity of the CdS nanosheet and measured the hydrogen production rate as 41.1 mmol h−1 g−1. Doping and defect formation play a crucial role in modulating electronic structure, which manifested in the catalytic activity of zinc indium sulfide [29–32]: oxygen-doped ZnIn
2
S
4
nanosheet shows 4.5 times higher hydrogen evolution rate (
2120
μ m
ol h−1 g−1) than that of pristine material [33]. However, the quantum efficiency is not too high for large-scale production of hydrogen at the industrial level. Rapid recombination of the photogenerated electron and hole is the key challenge to improve the efficiency of photocatalysts. To get rid of this problem, scientists have designed heterostructures, which allow spatial separation of charge carriers, leading to an enhancement in exciton lifetime [16,34–37]. A remarkable improvement in quantum yield and hydrogen evolution rate has been observed for MoS
2
upon heterostructure fabrication [38–43]. Similar enhancement has also been noticed in the case of g-C
3
N
4
for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [44–47]. The excellent performance of MoS
2
-based heterostructures for solar-driven water splitting stimulated experimentalists to explore the potentiality of other transition metal dichalcogenides [48–51]. A remarkable hydrogen evolution activity with the rate of 209.79 mmol h−1 g−1 is achieved for CdS/WS
2
-MoS
2
nanocomposite [49].

Quantum dots are emerging materials in this exciting field of research because of tunable absorption [52–64]. Jindal et al. [52] have investigated the catalytic activity of haeckelite boron nitride quantum dot for hydrogen evolution. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are functionalized with oxygen, borinic acid, and boric acid to unveil the effect of functionalization on the catalytic activity relevant to hydrogen evolution reaction [54]. Yan et al. [55] have demonstrated functionalization techniques to systematically tune the HOMO-LUMO gap of GQDs, which leads to intramolecular Z-scheme structure, resulting efficient photocatalytic water splitting activity under visible light. Composite systems having quantum dots show enhanced photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution from water splitting [57–60,62]. Dinda et al. [64] have synthesized dye-sensitized graphene quantum dot, which show promising photocatalytic hydrogen evolution ability. The heterojunction between black and red phosphorus quantum dots forms a Z-scheme system, which can be used for photocatalytic water splitting [56]. Zhou et al. [63] have proposed black and blue phosphorus quantum dots for photocatalytic water splitting. p-n type nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots are capable of accelerating overall water splitting under visible light [53]. In this study, the solar-driven water splitting activity of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) is explored with the aim of utilizing a wide region of the visible light spectrum to enhance the yield of hydrogen. Four different types of N-doped environments, such as graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N, pyridinic-N, and pyrazole-N are introduced by Li et al. [65], and this study center over pyridinic-N GQDs. Two types of NGQDs, quasi-trigonal and hexagonal, are considered in this study as shown in Fig. 1.

All the first-principle calculations are performed using density functional theory (DFT) as implemented in the Vienna Ab initio simulation package (VASP) [66], which utilizes plane waves as a basis set. The interaction between the valence and core electrons is described by the projector augmented wave (PAW) potential [67]. The kinetic energy cut-off is set to 500 eV. The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) of Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof [68] (PBE) is chosen as the exchange–correlation functional for structural relaxation and thermodynamic property calculations. The remaining calculations are performed by Heyd–Scuseria–Ernzerhof [69] (HSE06) hybrid functional, which is exceptional to determine HOMO-LUMO gap, can be expressed as:
(1)
E
x c
H S E
= α
E
x
S R , H F
( ω )
+
( 1 − α )
E
x
S R , P B E
( ω )
+
E
x
L R , P B E
( ω )
+
E
c
P B E
,
where ω and α (=1/4) are the range separation parameter and HF mixing constant, respectively. E
x
S R , H F
is the short-range HF exchange, whereas E
x
S R , P B E
and E
x
L R , P B E
are the short-range and long-range components of the PBE exchange functional, respectively. E
c
P B E
is the PBE correlation energy.

The convergence criteria for energy and force are set to 10
− 6
eV
and 0.02 eV/Å. A large vacuum region (box dimension = 30 Å × 30 Å × 20 Å) of more than 15 Å is added in three directions to avoid interaction between periodic images. The width of quantum dots along X-direction is 13.28 Å, while it varies from 9.51 to 13.19 Å along Y-direction. The thermodynamic property calculations are performed by the density functional perturbation theory [70] (DFPT) as implemented in the PHONOPY code [71]. The atomic structures are analyzed by VESTA code [72]. The frequency-dependent dielectric function is calculated to determine the optical absorption spectra. The imaginary part of the dielectric function is computed using the following equation:
(2)
ϵ
2
( ω )
=
2
e
2
π
Ω
ϵ
0
∑
o , u
| <
ψ
u
|
p
|
ψ
o
> |
2
δ
(
E
u
−
E
o
− E )
where ω , Ω , p, o, and u represent the frequency of incident radiation, the unit cell volume, the polarization induced by the electric field, and the occupied and unoccupied states, respectively.

This study considers two types of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots: quasi-trigonal and hexagonal. Next, the size of these quantum dots is increased irrespective of shape to model different NGQDs. The presence of nitrogen atoms increases the electron density in quantum dots. Thus, such kind of doping can be designated as n-doping. The optimized structure of the smallest studied NGQD of each type is shown in Fig. 1. The quantum dots are completely planer, which is obvious because of conjugation between all the atoms except hydrogen. It is to be noted that nitrogen atoms are sp
2
hybridized, and its lone pair of electrons reside in one of the sp
2
orbitals pointing towards the center of the cavity [73]. The diameter of the cavity is 3.90 Å. Such type of cavity is suitable for accommodating transition metal as the cavity is encompassed by four nitrogen atoms, having a lone pair of electrons each [74,75]. The nitrogen sites generally act as catalytically active sites for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [76], maybe due to the lone pair of electrons on nitrogen, which can capture proton. On the other hand, carbon atoms in six-member rings like benzene can function as catalytically active sites for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) [77]. Thus, from the point of view of structure, these systems have the potentiality to act as catalysts for overall water splitting.

The density of states of the smallest studied quantum dot is shown in Fig. 2, which reveals that the quantum dot is a semiconductor with a HOMO-LUMO gap of around 2.97 eV. This finding is in good agreement with the previous study on graphene quantum dots [78]. The states around the Fermi level are mainly π -type as those originating from the
p
z
orbitals of carbon and nitrogen as indicated by the projected density of states. However, the lone pair of electrons of nitrogen have a significant contribution to some of the frontier occupied states. As the HOMO-LUMO gap satisfies the requirement of band gap of 1.23 eV for photocatalytic water splitting, we step forward to explore this material for solar-driven hydrogen evolution by varying the size of quantum dot. The goal behind size variation is to utilize different regions of the visible light spectrum to enhance the hydrogen yield.

Next, the electronic properties of NGQDs are studied with the help of screened range-separated hybrid functional, HSE06. The alignment of frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) with respect to the redox potential of water is shown in Fig. 3. The redox potential depends on pH [79]. Hence, the oxidation potential (−5.67 eV w.r.t vacuum) and the reduction potential (−4.44 eV w.r.t vacuum) of water are shifted following the equations:[79]
E
H
+
/
H
2
r e d
= − 4 . 44 + 0 . 059 × p H
and
E
O
2
/
H
2
O
o x
= − 5 . 67 + 0 . 059 × p H
. As can be seen from the figure, NGQDs satisfy the essential criteria to be applied for overall water splitting: FMOs straddle the redox potential of water, i.e., the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) lies below the oxidation potential and the lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) lies above the reduction potential. Such an alignment is suitable for the photoexcited electron to reduce H
+
to H
2
and the photogenerated hole to oxidize H
2
O to O
2
, implying that NGQDs are capable of overall photocatalytic water splitting. The HOMO shifts towards higher energy, while the LUMO overall goes down in energy with increase in size of NGQDs, resulting decrease in the HOMO-LUMO gap. This is consistent with the quantum confinement effect. The HOMO-LUMO gap varies from 3.01 to 2.32 eV, which implies that NGQDs can absorb visible light of the solar spectrum. Thus, these quantum dots are eligible for visible-light-driven overall water splitting. We have studied the effect of doping concentration for NGQD(28) by reducing the number of nitrogen atom from 4 to 2 and noticed that the HOMO-LUMO gap changes from 2.98 to 2.91 eV.

The photoinduced electrons and holes need to provide adequate energy to accelerate the redox reaction of water. The driving force for photocatalytic water splitting comes from the photogenerated electron at LUMO and the hole at HOMO [80]. To make sure whether light-induced excitons are able to drive the redox reaction of water or not, the Gibbs free energy change for the formation of intermediates is calculated following Nørskov et al. [81]:
(3)
Δ G = Δ E + Δ Z P E − T Δ S +
G
U
+
G
p H
where
Δ E
is the reaction energy at the DFT level, Δ ZPE and Δ S are the differences in zero-point energy and entropy, respectively. T is the temperature at the absolute scale, 298 K, in this work. The effect of light-induced potential (U) is considered through G
U
= -eU. The free energy correction because of H
+
ions is taken into account as G
p H
= k
B
T ln10 × pH. Prior to determining the free energy changes, it is important to introduce the intermediates, which leads to overall water splitting. According to Nørskov et al. [81], the OER proceeds through the following steps:
(4)
∗ + H
2
O =
∗ OH +
H
+
+
e
−
(5)
∗ OH =
∗ O +
H
+
+
e
−
(6)
∗ O + H
2
O =
∗ OOH +
H
+
+
e
−
(7)
∗ OOH =
∗ +
O
2
+
H
+
+
e
−
where *A represents species adsorbed on the surface of NGQD(28)[*] and the corresponding intermediates are shown in Fig. 4(a). The carbon atoms (S
1
and S
2
) bonded with the nitrogen atoms are electron deficient because of the difference in electronegativity. So, OH gets adsorbed on the carbon atom (S
1
) adjacent to nitrogen, which undergoes epoxide and peroxide formation in the second and third step, respectively. Finally, oxygen evolves from peroxide moiety, regenerating the catalyst. It is to be noted that the oxidation in the above four steps is accomplished with light-induced holes, while the photogenerated electron carries out the reduction of H
+
. The HER takes place through the following steps:
(8)
∗ +
H
+
+
e
−
=
∗ H
(9)
∗ H +
H
+
+
e
−
=
∗ +
H
2
where a nitrogen atom captures hydrogen (H) in the first step (Fig. 4(b)) and hydrogen (H
2
) is produced in the second step with the addition of another hydrogen (H). (H
+
+ e
−
) is considered as 1/2H
2
.

Next, the Gibbs free energy change for the aforementioned steps is calculated with and without light-induced potential on the surface of NGQD(28) at pH= 7 and shown in Fig. 5. The figure reveals that all the steps of OER are endergonic without light-induced potential (U=0); 1.56, 1.70, 3.65, and 1.66 eV for the first to fourth steps, respectively. The energy barrier is maximum (1.95 eV) for the formation of *OOH, indicating the rate-determining step for oxygen evolution. The same rate-limiting step is identified for other materials [76,82,83]. The energy barrier for the rate-controlling step, as mentioned above, exactly matches with that of β -SnSe and CHF-7 [82,83]. However, all the steps become energetically favorable in presence of light, −0.03, −1.49, −1.14, and −4.72 eV for the first to last step, respectively. This shows the capability of NGQD(28) for solar-driven oxygen evolution. It is to be noted that the free energy change is slightly negative (−0.03 eV) for *OH formation under solar light irradiation. On the other hand, HOMO shifts towards higher energy with increase in size of quantum dots, diminishing the light-induced potential. Thus, pH may need to increase for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution with larger size quantum dots. This interpretation is in great agreement with the study of Xie et al. [84] The adsorption of OH at the S
2
site (Fig. S1) is an endergonic process (Fig. 6) with the free energy change of 0.05 eV in presence of solar radiation. However, the Gibbs free energy change becomes negative (−0.01) with a slight increase (0.5) in pH. Thus, our explanation of pH enhancement is even justified for solar-driven oxygen evolution at the S
2
site of NGQD(28). On the other hand, hydrogen adsorption is energetically feasible without irradiation of solar light, but desorption is an endergonic process in absence of light. The Gibbs free energy change for hydrogen adsorption without irradiation-induced potential is −0.24 eV, which is very close to the hydrogen adsorption energy on Pt [85]. It is to be noted that close to zero hydrogen adsorption energy is the signatory characteristic of excellent hydrogen evolution ability. However, hydrogen desorption energy is 0.83 eV without light-induced potential, implying that hydrogen evolution is not possible without exposure to light. The desorption of hydrogen becomes energetically favorable (−1.95 eV) under solar-light irradiation, which indicates the capability of NGQD(28) for solar-driven hydrogen evolution.

An ideal photocatalyst should have optical absorption in the visible region of the solar spectrum. Therefore, the optical absorption spectrum of all the studied quantum dots is calculated and shown in Fig. 7. The figure shows that all the quantum dots absorb visible light: the lowest energy bright absorption peak around 3 eV for NGQD(28), NGQD(30), and NGQD(32) arises due to the absorption of visible light of around 408 nm wavelength. The higher energy absorption peak will appear under the exposure to UV light for these quantum dots. However, hexagonal or quasi-hexagonal quantum dots show wide and sharp absorption in between 2–3 eV, which indicates extensive utilization of solar energy spectrum by these quantum dots. The origin of these peaks is associated with some specific transitions, which can be analyzed by the density of states. Correlating the excitation energy with the density of states, it can be demonstrated that the lowest energy bright absorption peak originates from the HOMO to LUMO transition for the smallest studied quantum dot. Similarly, HOMO
⟶
LUMO+1 and HOMO
⟶
LUMO+2 transitions are responsible for the first and second higher energy absorption peaks.

We have studied nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for solar-driven hydrogen evolution. The frontier molecular orbitals are suitably placed with respect to the redox potential of water, which exposes that all the studied quantum dots are capable of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution at pH=7. The HOMO-LUMO gap varies with the size of quantum dots from 3.01 to 2.32 eV, indicating that these quantum dots can utilize visible light for solar-induced hydrogen evolution. The Gibbs free energy calculation reveals that oxygen and hydrogen evolution reactions are not feasible at normal conditions in absence of light. However, the photogenerated electron and hole make these reactions possible in presence of light. This study extrapolates that the pH of the medium may need to increase for hydrogen evolution with larger size quantum dots. All the quantum dots absorb visible light. The lowest energy bright absorption peak of NGQD(28) arises from the HOMO
⟶
LUMO transition. We strongly believe that our study will stimulate experimentalists for the real-field application of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for the extensive production of hydrogen.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: B.M. is grateful to the Department of Atomic Energy, Government of India for the financial support. We strongly acknowledge the Kalinga Cluster of NISER Bhubaneswar for providing enormous computational support.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The optimized structure of remaining quantum dots; Frontier molecular orbital energies and the energy gap between them; Schematic representation of OER at the S
2
site; The optical absorption spectra of undoped analogue of NGQDs; Electrostatic potential of NGQD(28).
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2023.11.328.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary material related to this article.
MMC S1
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle:
The In
2
SeS/g-C
3
N
4
